danger_signals.visual_form: Enemy sprites with distinct silhouettes (Bee form giant enemy, Monch intelligent monkey, helmeted humanoid, goat-like humanoid)
danger_signals.meaning: Combat threat; magic power drain on attack; potential item drops or poison hazard

danger_signals.visual_form: Closed door sprites, special brick barriers
danger_signals.meaning: Progression blocked; requires specific character abilities or items to proceed
Physical danger to player character; damage or status effect risk
Health-damaging resource; inverse of healing bread

danger_signals.visual_form: Large enemy sprites in designated boss chambers
danger_signals.meaning: Critical combat challenge; crown-guarding entity; required defeat to progress

safety_signals.visual_form: Home base with portrait of Jiela's great-great-grandfather Lark on wall; character select screen
safety_signals.meaning: Safe zone; character swap location; password save point; respite from dungeon
Resource acquisition (items, healing, magic restoration); temporary safe commercial space
Beneficial item acquisition; progression enabler; character-exclusive tools
Health and magic restoration; active sustenance during dungeon traverse
Door unlock enabler; progression facilitator; monster defeat reward

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Dragon Slayer sword (ultimate family weapon); four crowns (family objective markers)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Family household on surface with Lark portrait; character-specific magical items (gloves, wings, key, crossbow, mattock, crowns)

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Pochi the pet (described as 'actually a monster from the underground dungeon'); King Dragon Keela (final boss, magically entrapped in painting)
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Multiple enemy types with distinct silhouettes; unused sprites suggest environmental variants (Bee form of giant enemy implies ladder-climbing behavior state)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Five major dungeon sections with noticeably different visual characteristics; different background music per section; section-specific boss chambers

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Dungeon sections sealed with special bricks and locked doors; family household as primary visual anchor; surface-to-dungeon entry point
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player cycles between surface (character swap, password save) and dungeon (crown hunting); character abilities guide which section is accessible

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Dungeon sections progressively open as crowns are obtained; boss chamber defeated indicators; magical item accumulation shown in inventory/chest access
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Roas (young boy) gains ability to use crowns; family members must return to surface to swap and tackle new section types designed for specific character

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Dragon Slayer accessibility unlocked; final boss chamber (Keela in painting) becomes narratively active; underground labyrinth converges on painting location
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Roas uses crown magic to locate Dragon Slayer; wields sword to slay Keela; ending sequence shows family climbing ladder out of dungeon with walking and waving poses

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Endless monster supply; monster sprite variations per dungeon section; boss silhouettes larger and distinct from standard enemies
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Different monster groups in each section (groups 4 and 7 contain unused enemy types suggesting unused behavior states); magic power drain on player attack; item drops (keys, bread, potions, poison, money) signal combat outcome

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Brick walls require mattock (Lyll only); block puzzles require magical gloves (Xemn only); flight-required passages; magical key passages
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player must determine correct character for each crown; section design enforces family member usage across all characters; abilities are visible through item possession and character selection UI

environmental_storytelling.example: Five major dungeon sections each have noticeably different characteristics and different background music; each section designed with one particular playable character in mind, creating environmental narrative about which character belongs in which space

Lark (Jiela's great-great-grandfather) portrait on family home wall; unused portrait of unknown character; family poses (Royas climbing ladder, Pochi lying down/facing up, Douel and Jiela unseen in ending); visual genealogy on home wall establishes family legacy

Monster drops communicate reward type: keys unlock doors, bread restores health, potions restore magic, poison damages player, money enables commerce; visual sprite variation differentiates resource types

Magical items exclusive to characters (gloves, wings, key, crossbow, mattock) visible in inventory/on character; item acquisition signals which character should be deployed next; equipment visual presence indicates character specialization
Four crown-guarding bosses in designated chambers at section endpoints; final boss (Keela) in painting deep in labyrinth; chamber design and boss positioning establish progression hierarchy
Shops and inns located in impractical positions; locked doors and special brick barriers create maze navigation; visual architecture communicates puzzle complexity and resource management challenge

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Highest attack power; cannot jump as high as others; can use magical gloves to move special blocks; designed for combat-heavy sections and block puzzle areas
Relatively weak attributes; only character who can use magical wings, magical key, and crossbow; enables flight passages and locked door areas; essential for one crown acquisition
Weak attacks; only character who can use four crowns and wield Dragon Slayer sword; transforms to final boss handler; must be cycled to surface to activate crown magic
Can jump higher than family members; only character who can use mattock to destroy special blocks; equal attack power to Roas; designed for vertical obstacle sections
Powerful attacks with limited range; cannot jump high; can use few magical items; acts like dog but is actually dungeon monster; positioned as limited-utility character

Endless supply; attacks drain player magic power; drops keys, bread, potions, poison, or money; silhouettes vary by dungeon section; four unused sprites (Bee giant form, Monch monkey, helmeted humanoid, goat-like entity) suggest behavior variants tied to environmental context (ladder climbing)
Magically entrapped in underground painting; awaits Dragon Slayer confrontation; requires all four crowns to locate and defeat; visual presence in painting as narrative anchor
Characters return periodically to swap members and obtain password saves; safe zone between dungeon cycles; portrait gallery provides genealogical narrative context

notes: Legacy of the Wizard uses character-locked dungeon design as primary world-state manifestation—each family member's unique abilities gate access to specific dungeon sections, creating a visual and behavioral progression system where world state is communicated through accessible vs. inaccessible areas. The five major dungeon sections each have distinct visual characteristics and music, signaling character-section alignment. Boss chambers serve as visual endpoints for each section, creating hierarchical progression markers. The surface household acts as a visual safe-haven and character-swap hub, establishing spatial narrative of family quest. Unused enemy sprites (Bee form, Monch, helmeted figure, goat-like entity) and unused portraits suggest originally planned environmental or genealogical storytelling that was cut but remained in data. Regional differences (title screen brick recolor, credits localization) indicate minimal narrative/world-state changes between JP and US versions.
sources_used: tcrf (The Cutting Room Floor - Legacy of the Wizard)
wikipedia (Legacy of the Wizard - Wikipedia article)
